narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting their likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, LSAT score of 40, Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings both at 2, and your plan to attend full-time—and calculated an 84% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this prediction to prioritize admits likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The three most influential factors in pushing your prediction toward failure were your Year 3 decile ranking, your Year 1 decile ranking, and your LSAT score, in that order of impact. Your Year 3 decile of 2, well below the typical student's 5.5-ish ranking, had the strongest pull toward predicting failure, as it signals consistently lower performance relative to peers in later undergrad years. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 2, compared to the average around 5.7, reinforced this concern by highlighting early academic struggles that often persist. On a more positive note, your LSAT score of 40—higher than the typical 36.7—worked against the failure prediction, nudging the model toward seeing potential for success. While your full-time status aligns with most students (over 90%) and your GPA of 3.10 is close to average, these didn't outweigh the dominant influence of your lower decile rankings. Compared to typical admitted students, who often rank in the middle or higher deciles and show steady improvement, your profile stood out as riskier for bar passage. This isn't a judgment on your character or potential as a white male from a supportive background—it's a data-driven flag on academic trajectory. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through post-baccalaureate options or targeted LSAT prep to amplify that strength, then reapply with updated credentials showing upward momentum. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps, and with your solid LSAT foundation, you're well-positioned to shift the odds in your favor next time.
